skill.name: testing-patterns
skill.description: Cross-language guidance for unit, integration, end-to-end, TDD, mocking, test data, and CI testing patterns.
skill.summary: Plan better test suites with practical patterns for test design, mocking, data, coverage, and CI.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, credential, filesystem, network, and heuristic findings, but manual review shows almost all are documentation examples or parser false positives. The only confirmed executable behavior is a visible Bash coverage helper that runs pytest, which creates low operational risk when used on untrusted repositories. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Executable Coverage Script Runs Pytest
low_findings.description: The skill ships a Bash helper that invokes pytest with coverage options and forwards caller-provided pytest arguments. This is expected for a testing skill, but it executes project test code and should be used only in trusted repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is an executable shell script and directly runs pytest. The behavior is visible and aligned with the skill purpose, so the concern is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are Markdown code fences, diagrams, paths, or CI snippets. I did not find evidence that these examples execute automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is inside Markdown examples or explanatory diagrams. No loader hook, hidden command, or automatic execution path was found in those locations.

low_findings.title: Credential And Environment References Are CI Placeholders
low_findings.description: DATABASE_URL and API_KEY appear in GitHub Actions examples for test services and GitHub secrets. No evidence found that the skill reads real environment variables or exfiltrates credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are confined to illustrative YAML snippets, including GitHub secrets placeholders. There is no executable code path that accesses or transmits these values.

low_findings.title: Filesystem And Network References Are Testing Guidance
low_findings.description: The filesystem and network detections are examples for cache paths, fixture loading, mocking boundaries, and contract testing. No evidence found of hidden file harvesting or unauthorized outbound network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are normal test documentation and CI examples. The only executable file reviewed is the coverage script, and it does not contain network or file collection logic.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Flags Are Parser False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography flags map to Python keyword expansion such as **overrides, not to cryptographic APIs. The reconnaissance flags map to test fixture examples and directory trees, not system discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged lines found ordinary Python test-data examples and Markdown metadata. No weak hash, cipher use, host probing, or network scanning command appears there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Executable Coverage Script Runs Pytest
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill ships a Bash helper that invokes pytest with coverage options and forwards caller-provided pytest arguments. This is expected for a testing skill, but it executes project test code and should be used only in trusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file is an executable shell script and directly runs pytest. The behavior is visible and aligned with the skill purpose, so the concern is operational rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: Teams often mix slow tests, brittle mocks, and unclear coverage goals. This skill provides practical patterns for designing reliable tests, test data, mocks, and CI feedback loops.
actual_capabilities: Explains unit, integration, and end-to-end test tradeoffs using a test pyramid model.
Provides TDD workflow guidance with red, green, and refactor steps.
Describes mocking, stubbing, spying, fakes, and dependency injection boundaries.
Shows fixture, factory, builder, and object mother patterns for test data.
Outlines CI test stages, parallelization, retries, reports, coverage, and secret handling.
Includes a Bash helper for running pytest coverage checks with a threshold.
limitations: Examples are mostly Python and GitHub Actions, though the concepts are language agnostic.
The coverage helper assumes pytest, pytest-cov, and a src directory layout.
It does not automatically inspect a repository or generate a complete test suite.
Framework-specific commands may need adjustment for JavaScript, Java, Go, or other stacks.

use_cases.title: Design a Balanced Test Suite
use_cases.description: Choose which behavior belongs in unit, integration, or end-to-end tests before adding new coverage.

use_cases.title: Standardize Testing Guidance
use_cases.description: Create shared rules for naming tests, using mocks, managing fixtures, and avoiding brittle checks.

use_cases.title: Improve CI Test Feedback
use_cases.description: Plan pipeline stages for linting, unit tests, integration tests, reports, coverage gates, and flaky test handling.

prompt_templates.title: Classify My Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my feature description and suggest which checks should be unit, integration, or end-to-end tests. Explain the tradeoffs briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Plan TDD Cases
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new behavior with TDD
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a red-green-refactor plan for this behavior. List the first failing tests, the smallest passing implementation, and refactor checks.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Mock Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this testing approach and identify what should be mocked, faked, or tested with real dependencies. Focus on boundaries and behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Design CI Quality Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI testing strategy for this repository. Include stages, parallelization, coverage reporting, flaky test handling, and secret safety.

output_examples.input: A developer asks where to add tests for a new checkout discount rule.
output: Unit tests cover discount calculations and edge cases.
Integration tests cover cart, pricing, and persistence boundaries.
End-to-end tests cover one successful checkout journey only.

output_examples.input: A team has tests that mock every internal method.
output: Keep real domain objects for business logic checks.
Mock only external services such as payment, email, and inventory APIs.
Use fixtures or builders to reduce repeated setup.

output_examples.input: A CI pipeline is slow and often flakes on browser tests.
output: Run linting and unit tests before slower stages.
Shard long test groups and retry only known flaky tests.
Publish test and coverage reports for pull request review.

best_practices: Start with behavior and risk, then choose the smallest test type that gives useful confidence.
Mock external boundaries, but keep core business logic real in most tests.
Keep CI feedback fast by ordering cheap checks before slow integration and end-to-end tests.
anti_patterns: Do not mock the code under test or duplicate production logic inside assertions.
Do not make tests depend on execution order or data created by earlier tests.
Do not run sensitive integration tests without isolated credentials and clear approval controls.

faq.question: Does this skill write tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that help an assistant design or review tests.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: The guidance is language agnostic, but many examples use Python and pytest.

faq.question: Can it help with TDD?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes red, green, and refactor workflow guidance with example test case sequencing.

faq.question: Does it include CI guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers pipeline stages, parallel tests, retries, reports, coverage uploads, and secret handling.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. The skill content does not require network access, though CI examples mention external services.

faq.question: What should users review before running the script?
faq.answer: Users should run the coverage script only in trusted repositories because it executes pytest and project test code.
